title: Christian Kingdoms
subtitle: 718–1230 · Asturias to Las Navas de Tolosa

categories.label: Power
categories.subtitle: From mountain refuge to multi-kingdom Iberia

entries.label: Pelayo — founder of Asturias
entries.hint: Pelayo (Pelagius) founds the Kingdom of Asturias after defeating a Muslim force at Covadonga (c.722) — the battle is later magnified into the founding moment of the Reconquista; the earliest primary sources are from Alfonso III's court (866–910), 150 years later; Covadonga is as much myth as history.
entries.note: Chronicle of Alfonso III (c.910): primary source — but 150 years after the events, ideologically motivated. The Battle of Covadonga: dated c.718–722 in scholarship (Collins, Reconquista debate). Britannica: 'the stories and relics of Pelayo belong to legend rather than fact; it was in this legendary guise that he became an important symbol'. Linehan (1993), History and the Historians of Medieval Spain: critiques the source tradition.

entries.label: Alfonso II — the Chaste and Santiago de Compostela
entries.hint: Alfonso II builds the original Santiago de Compostela cathedral over the supposed tomb of St James — the discovery is traditionally dated to 813 but most historians place it 820–830; the earliest surviving royal charter endorsing the tomb dates to 829 or 834; Alfonso makes the first royal journey from Oviedo and establishes the cult that becomes medieval Europe's most important pilgrimage.
entries.note: Alfonso II's construction programme: documented in chronicles. The discovery date: traditionally 813 but most historians place it 820–830 — the earliest surviving charter (829 or 834) is the documentary anchor. Moralejo (1985) on Asturian architecture. The first royal pilgrimage: documented in chronicles as Alfonso II's journey from Oviedo. Linehan (1993): sceptical analysis of the source tradition. Santiago Cathedral: UNESCO World Heritage (1985).

entries.label: Alfonso VI — Toledo reconquered (1085)
entries.hint: Alfonso VI of León and Castile reconquers Toledo on 25 May 1085 — the first major Muslim city taken in the Reconquista, the old Visigothic capital; the Christian world celebrates; the shocked Muslim taifas invite the Almoravids from Morocco, who reverse many gains.
entries.note: The reconquest of Toledo 25 May 1085: documented in both Christian chronicles and Arabic sources. The Almoravid invitation: ibn Idhari, al-Bayan al-Mughrib. Alfonso VI's title 'Emperor of all Spain': documented in royal charters (Archivo de la Catedral de Toledo). El Cid's relationship with Alfonso VI: documented in the Chronica Adefonsi Imperatoris. Barton & Fletcher (2000), The World of El Cid.

entries.label: Alfonso VIII — Las Navas de Tolosa (1212)
entries.hint: Alfonso VIII leads a coalition of Castile, Aragon, Navarre and Portugal to decisive victory over the Almohad Caliphate at Las Navas de Tolosa on 16 July 1212 — the turning point of the Reconquista; within 40 years Córdoba, Seville and Valencia fall; the Almohad empire collapses.
entries.note: The battle 16 Jul 1212: documented in Archbishop Rodrigo Jiménez de Rada, De Rebus Hispaniae (c.1243) — primary Castilian source; he was present. Arabic sources: Ibn Abi Zar, Rawdh al-Qirtas. Alfonso VIII's correspondence with Pope Innocent III requesting a crusade: Vatican Archives. Lomax (1978), The Reconquista of Spain: the standard English study.

entries.label: Ferdinand III — the 1230 union that ends this era
entries.hint: When his uncle Alfonso IX of León dies in 1230, Ferdinand III inherits both crowns — the permanent union of Castile and León ends seventy years of dynastic rivalry; it is the foundational political act that enables the rapid reconquest of Córdoba (1236) and Seville (1248) within the next two decades.
entries.note: The union of Castile and León 1230: Treaty of Benavente — documented in royal archives. The preceding split: Alfonso VII's division between Sancho III (Castile) and Fernando II (León) in 1157 — documented in his testament. González (1980), Fernando III el Santo Vol.I: the standard biography. The union's immediate military consequence: Fernando now commands the combined resources of both kingdoms for the southern campaigns.

entries.label: Sancho III the Great — and the birth of Castile and Aragon
entries.hint: Sancho III of Pamplona is the most powerful Christian monarch in early 11th-century Iberia — his hegemony extends over Castile, Aragon, Sobrarbe and Ribagorza; at his death in 1035 he divides his realm between sons, making Castile an independent kingdom (García) and Aragon a kingdom (Ramiro I) for the first time; the partition directly creates the political geography of medieval Spain.
entries.note: Sancho III's partition: his testament (1035) — AHN. The creation of the Kingdom of Castile: García receives Castile as a kingdom (not a county). The creation of the Kingdom of Aragon: Ramiro I. Menéndez Pidal (1950), La España del Cid: contextualises Sancho's hegemony. Linehan (1993), History and the Historians of Medieval Spain: the standard critical survey. Sancho III's importance: without this partition, Castile and Aragon do not exist as the political units that shape subsequent Iberian history.

categories.label: Conflict
categories.subtitle: The Reconquista — more complex than the legend

entries.label: Battle of Covadonga — the founding myth
entries.hint: Pelayo's victory at Covadonga — the scale is disputed: the Chronicle of 754 (the most contemporary Christian source) does not mention Covadonga, Pelayo, or the battle at all; it is entirely silent on the Asturian rebellion. The description of a minor skirmish ('thirty wild asses in the hills') comes from later Arabic chronicles such as Ibn Hayyan; the Asturian chronicles of Alfonso III's court (c.910) then inflate it into a providential founding.
entries.note: Chronicle of 754 (Mozarabic Chronicle): primary — silent on Covadonga entirely. The 'wild asses' description: Arabic chronicles (al-Maqqari drawing on Ibn Hayyan) — not the Chronicle of 754 as commonly misattributed. Chronicle of Alfonso III (c.910): inflates it as a providential founding — 150 years after the event. Collins (1989), The Arab Conquest of Spain: analyses the source discrepancy carefully. Linehan (1993), History and the Historians of Medieval Spain ch.1: the standard critique of the source tradition.

entries.label: El Cid takes Valencia (1094)
entries.hint: Rodrigo Díaz de Vivar (El Cid) captures Valencia in June 1094 and rules it independently until his death in 1099 — a Christian warrior sometimes fighting for Muslim rulers against Christians; his career is the clearest example of the Reconquista's complexity; his widow Jimena holds Valencia two more years.
entries.note: Carmen Campidoctoris (c.1090s): the earliest Latin poem about El Cid — near-contemporary evidence. Historia Roderici (c.1110): the primary Latin biography. The Cantar de Mio Cid (c.1207): the literary account — manuscript Biblioteca Nacional de España, Vitr.7-17. Fletcher (1989), The Quest for El Cid: uses the Historia Roderici as the primary source. Valencia falls back to the Almoravids 1102 — three years after El Cid's death.

entries.label: Battle of Las Navas de Tolosa — the turning point
entries.loc: Las Navas de Tolosa, Jaén (Sierra Morena)
entries.hint: The combined Christian kingdoms defeat the Almohad Caliph al-Nasir — estimates of dead range from 2,000 to 100,000 (sources vary wildly); within a generation the Almohad empire collapses; the battle opens Andalusia to Christian reconquest.
entries.note: Archbishop Rodrigo Jiménez de Rada, De Rebus Hispaniae (c.1243): eyewitness account — he was present and coordinated the coalition. Pope Innocent III's letter of congratulation: Vatican Archives. Arabic sources: Ibn Abi Zar. Casualty figures: impossible to verify — the wildly varying numbers document medieval tendencies in military reporting. The captured Almohad standard: reportedly sent to Las Huelgas monastery, Burgos (where it survives).

categories.label: Order
categories.subtitle: Fueros, royal charters and the development of Iberian law

entries.label: Fueros — the municipal charters of Christian Spain
entries.hint: The fueros (municipal charters) granted to repopulated frontier towns from the 11th century onwards give towns significant autonomy and legal rights — the Fuero of León (1017/1020) is among the earliest; they are the primary legal evidence for the organisation of Reconquista society.
entries.note: Fuero of León (1017/1020): Archivo de la Catedral de León — primary text, digitised. Fuero de Jaca (c.1063): also early. García de Valdeavellano (1968), Historia de España Vol.I: the standard survey of the fuero system. The fueros' long-term importance: the Cádiz Constitution debates (1810) explicitly invoke the fuero tradition. They are among the earliest documents of representative governance in Western Europe.

entries.label: Santiago de Compostela Cathedral — the Romanesque primary source
entries.hint: Construction of the Cathedral of Santiago de Compostela begins in 1075 under Bishop Diego Peláez and the Pilgrim's Guide (c.1139) is the first European tourist guide — documenting the pilgrimage routes, shrines and hostels; the Codex Calixtinus (c.1160) contains the earliest polyphonic Western music.
entries.note: UNESCO World Heritage (1985). Building begun 1075: documented in the Chronicon Compostellanum. Pilgrim's Guide (Liber V of Codex Calixtinus, c.1139): BnF and Archivo de la Catedral de Santiago — the first European travel guide. The Codex Calixtinus (c.1160): Archivo de la Catedral de Santiago — stolen 2011, recovered 2012. Moralejo et al. (1992), Liber Sancti Jacobi: the standard edition.

entries.label: Alfonso VII's partition — the Castilian-Leonese split (1157)
entries.hint: When Alfonso VII 'the Emperor' dies in 1157 he divides his realm between his two sons — Castile to Sancho III and León to Fernando II; the resulting 73-year separation produces rival kingdoms, border wars, distinct legal traditions, and the complex dynastic manoeuvring that culminates in the 1230 reunion under Ferdinand III.
entries.note: Alfonso VII's testament (1157): documented in royal archive. The subsequent border wars between Castile and León: documented in chronicles. The legal divergence: separate fueros, separate Cortes, separate military commands — all documented in AHN. Linehan (1993): analyses the split's long-term consequences. The 73-year separation is the essential context for understanding both the Battle of Las Navas (a joint effort despite rivalry) and the 1230 union's significance.

entries.label: Basque and Navarrese fueros — self-governing charters before Castile
entries.hint: The Basque provinces and Navarre maintain their own fueros (ancestral charters) independent of Castilian law — separate fiscal systems, customs borders, and regional assemblies including the General Assembly under the Tree of Guernica; their self-governing institutions predate and persist alongside the Christian kingdoms' centralising tendencies.
entries.note: The Fuero of Vizcaya (Fuero Viejo, c.1452 compilation of older custom): Archivo Foral de Bizkaia. The Navarrese Fuero General (13th c. compilation): Archivo General de Navarra. The General Assembly of Vizcaya under the Tree of Guernica: documented from 13th century — Archivo Foral. Monreal Zia (1977), Las instituciones públicas del señorío de Vizcaya: the standard study. García-Gallo (1956), 'El derecho foral como derecho regional': the comparative legal study. The fueros' survival through the Carlist Wars into the late 19th century: documented — their abolition (1876) is the grievance that drives Basque nationalism.

categories.label: Belief
categories.subtitle: The pilgrimage, the military orders and the Iberian Church

entries.label: The Camino de Santiago — pilgrimage as geopolitical act
entries.loc: Routes from France through Iberia to Santiago de Compostela
entries.hint: The discovery of St James's tomb c.830 creates the most important pilgrimage route in medieval Europe — by the 11th century 500,000 pilgrims annually; the route brings French Romanesque architecture, monastic reform (Cluny), new settlement patterns and pan-European cultural contact.
entries.note: The discovery narrative: Alfonso II's letter (c.830). The Pilgrim's Guide c.1139: first documented tourist guide in Europe (Liber V of Codex Calixtinus). Pilgrimage numbers: Valiña Sampedro (1971) estimates. The Cluniac reform connection: documented in papal correspondence (Vatican Archives). The route's physical evidence: documented archaeological finds along the Camino. UNESCO World Heritage (1993).

entries.label: Military orders — knights of Santiago, Calatrava, Alcántara
entries.hint: Three Iberian military orders are founded in 60 years — the Order of Calatrava (1158), Order of Santiago (1170) and Order of Alcántara (c.1176); they combine monastery and castle, hold frontier zones, and become major landowners; their archives are primary sources for the Reconquista's social organisation.
entries.note: Order of Calatrava: founded by Abbot Raimundo of Fitero (1158), confirmed by Pope Alexander III (1164) — papal bull in Vatican Archives. Order of Santiago: papal confirmation 1175 — Vatican Archives. O'Callaghan (1975), The Spanish Military Order of Calatrava: the standard study. The orders' archives: Archivo Histórico Nacional, Madrid (Sección de Órdenes Militares).

categories.label: Expression
categories.subtitle: Romanesque art, the Cantar de Mio Cid and Mozarab manuscripts

entries.label: Beatus of Liébana manuscripts — Christian art under Islam
entries.loc: Monasteries in Christian north; copied in Mozarab south
entries.hint: The illuminated Beatus manuscripts — commentaries on the Apocalypse by Beatus of Liébana (c.776, Asturian monk) — are copied and painted in the scriptoria of the Northern Christian Kingdoms (San Miguel de Escalada, Tábara, Valeránica); their vivid colours and flat figures document a Christian artistic tradition shaped by Mozarabic aesthetic influence; they are the primary art of the Christian north, not of Al-Andalus.
entries.note: UNESCO Memory of the World (2015). The Morgan Beatus (c.940–945): Pierpont Morgan Library, New York (MS M.644) — made at San Miguel de Escalada, León. The Girona Beatus (975): Cathedral Archive of Girona — made at the monastery of Tábara, Zamora. Williams (1994–2003), The Illustrated Beatus: A Corpus (5 vols): the complete catalogue. The manuscripts' northern Christian origin: the scriptoria of León and Castile, not Al-Andalus. The Mozarabic aesthetic influence: documented in Williams — eastern Mediterranean visual language absorbed through contact with Mozarab refugees from the south.

entries.label: Santa María del Naranco (848) — Ramiro I's pre-Romanesque palace
entries.hint: Santa María del Naranco was built by Ramiro I of Asturias (r.842–850), not Alfonso I — the dedication stone altar inside the building dates its completion to June 848; originally a royal hall converted to a church, it is the finest surviving pre-Romanesque building in Spain.
entries.note: Extant (UNESCO 1985). Dedication stone altar: in situ — dates completion to June 848 during Ramiro I's reign. Schlunk & Berenguer (1957), La pintura mural asturiana: the standard architectural study. The Alfonso I confusion: a common error in older literature; the dedicatory evidence clearly assigns the building to Ramiro I. The palace-to-church conversion: documented in later medieval sources.

entries.label: Codex Calixtinus — earliest three-part polyphony and the pilgrimage songs
entries.loc: Santiago de Compostela (Archivo de la Catedral)
entries.hint: The Codex Calixtinus (c.1160) contains 20 polyphonic compositions including Congaudeant catholici — the earliest surviving example of three-part (triplum) polyphony in Western music; earlier polyphony exists (Musica enchiriadis c.900; Winchester Troper c.1000) but the Codex Calixtinus is the earliest source of three independent vocal parts.
entries.note: HARD ANCHOR: Codex Calixtinus (c.1160): Archivo de la Catedral de Santiago de Compostela — returned after theft and recovery 2011–2012. Moralejo et al. (1992), Liber Sancti Jacobi: critical edition. Congaudeant catholici: identified as the earliest three-part polyphony in Western music — not the earliest polyphony overall (the Musica enchiriadis c.900 and Winchester Troper c.1000 are earlier). Stones (2011), A Manuscript of the Codex Calixtinus: the standard musicological study.

entries.label: Pórtico de la Gloria — the pinnacle of Romanesque sculpture
entries.hint: Master Mateo's Pórtico de la Gloria (completed 1188) is the absolute pinnacle of Romanesque sculpture in Europe — three arched portals with over 200 carved figures showing the Last Judgement; the transition from rigid Romanesque formalism to the naturalism of Gothic sculpture is visible in a single composition; the central column shows Mateo himself kneeling, with his self-portrait still visible.
entries.note: HARD ANCHOR: Pórtico de la Gloria (completed 1188): in situ, Santiago de Compostela Cathedral. The dedication inscription: dated 1 April 1188, documenting completion under King Ferdinand II of León and Master Mateo's authorship. Castiñeiras (2011), El Pórtico de la Gloria: the standard modern study. Master Mateo's stone self-portrait (the 'Santo dos Croques'): documented. The recent restoration (Fundación Pedro Barrié de la Maza, 2018): revealed original polychrome paint. UNESCO World Heritage (part of Cathedral, 1985).

entries.label: Cantar de Mio Cid — the first Spanish epic
entries.loc: San Pedro de Cardeña monastery (transmission); Burgos
entries.hint: The Cantar de Mio Cid is the earliest surviving major literary text in Castilian Spanish — the single surviving physical manuscript dates to the 14th century (c.1320–1330); the colophon records the date 1207 (Era 1245) as when Per Abbat copied a lost archetype, not as the date of the surviving manuscript.
entries.note: The surviving manuscript (14th c., c.1320–1330): Biblioteca Nacional de España, Vitr.7-17. The colophon date 1207: Per Abbat's copy of a lost original — the surviving manuscript is a copy of that copy. Menéndez Pidal (1908, 1944): the standard critical editions. Smith (1983), The Making of the Poema de Mio Cid: the standard English study. The poem's historical reliability: it post-dates El Cid by ~100 years and the surviving manuscript by a further century.

categories.label: Thought
categories.subtitle: The Toledo school of translators — Arabic to Latin

entries.label: Neogothicism — the ideology of the Reconquista
entries.loc: Oviedo (Alfonso III's court); later Toledo and León
entries.hint: The Chronicle of Alfonso III (c.910) constructs Neogothicism — the doctrine that the Asturian kings are the direct heirs to the Visigothic Kingdom of Toledo, giving them a providential duty to 'restore' lost Hispania; this ideology is the theological and political engine of the Reconquista, distinct from mere territorial defence.
entries.note: Chronicle of Alfonso III (c.910): Real Academia de la Historia — the primary document of Neogothic ideology. The Visigothic genealogy claim: Pelayo presented as a Gothic nobleman. The 'restoration' language: documented throughout the chronicle. Linehan (1993), History and the Historians of Medieval Spain ch.1–2: the standard critique of Neogothicism as constructed ideology. O'Callaghan (2003), Reconquest and Crusade: analyses Neogothicism alongside Crusading ideology as the two theological frameworks of the Reconquista.

entries.label: Toledo school of translators — Arabic science reaches Europe
entries.hint: After Toledo's reconquest (1085) a wave of European scholars come to translate Arabic philosophy and science into Latin — Aristotle, Ptolemy, Avicenna, al-Zahrawi, al-Khwarizmi; Gerard of Cremona alone translates 87 works; this transmission is how ancient Greek thought reaches medieval Europe.
entries.note: Gerard of Cremona (c.1114–1187): translated 87 Arabic works including Ptolemy's Almagest — documented in his obituary notice by his pupils (published Burnett 2001). Adelard of Bath's translations (c.1116–1142): documented. Burnett (2001), 'The Coherence of the Arabic-Latin Translation Program in Toledo' in Science in Context 14: the standard modern study. The physical manuscripts of translations: BnF and British Library hold early copies.

categories.label: Economy
categories.subtitle: Repopulation, frontier economy and the Camino's commercial impact

entries.label: Repopulation — the demographic engine of the Reconquista
entries.hint: The Christian kingdoms systematically repopulate conquered territory — granting fueros to attract settlers to dangerous frontier zones; the 10th–12th century sees the Duero valley, then the Tagus valley, then Extremadura settled; repopulation documents are primary sources for medieval demographic and economic history.
entries.note: Repopulation charters (cartas de población): Archivo de la Catedral de León; Archivo Histórico Nacional. The fuero system's role in repopulation: documented in the charters. Powers (1988), A Society Organized for War: The Iberian Municipal Militias: the standard study of frontier society. The population movement's scale: documented in charter density maps by Barton (2004), The Aristocracy in Twelfth-Century León and Castile.

entries.label: Parias system — taifa gold finances Romanesque cathedrals
entries.hint: The parias system — annual protection tributes paid in Fatimid gold dinars by the fragmented taifa kingdoms to the northern Christian kings — is the defining economic mechanism of 11th-century Iberia; it transfers immense wealth northward, finances the construction of Romanesque cathedrals (Santiago, León), pays for mercenary armies, and systematically drains the taifas, making their eventual conquest both affordable and inevitable.
entries.note: The parias payments: documented in the taifa rulers' court records (partially surviving) and in the Christian royal archives (Archivo de la Catedral de Toledo and AHN). Reilly (1988), The Kingdom of León-Castilla under King Alfonso VI: analyses the parias system in detail. The funding of Santiago Cathedral: the connection between parias revenue and Romanesque building programmes documented in Bishko (1969). The paradox: taifa sophistication finances the military-cultural apparatus of its own destruction.

entries.label: Parias — the taifa gold tribute that financed the Reconquista
entries.hint: After the caliphate's collapse, the 23 taifa kingdoms pay massive annual gold tribute (parias) to Christian kings for protection — this historic transfer of Islamic dinar wealth finances the construction of Romanesque cathedrals, pays European mercenary armies, and systematically drains the taifas; the parias system makes Christian expansion economically inevitable before it is militarily decisive.
entries.note: Parias documentation: Archivo de la Catedral de Toledo and AHN — royal parias agreements documented in charters. Reilly (1988), The Kingdom of León-Castilla under Alfonso VI: the standard study using the charter evidence. The connection to cathedral construction: explicitly argued by Bishko (1969) — Cluny donations funded by parias. Wasserstein (1985) on taifa fiscal weakness. The parias system: the most important medieval fiscal transfer in Iberian history before American silver.

categories.label: Exchange
categories.subtitle: The Camino as cultural highway and Toledo as translation hub

entries.label: Christian-Muslim alliances — the Reconquista's real complexity
entries.hint: Christian and Muslim rulers regularly ally against their co-religionists — El Cid fights for the Muslim king of Zaragoza, Alfonso VI makes alliances with Muslim taifas, Castilian and Aragonese kings negotiate with Almohads; the Reconquista is as much politics as religion.
entries.note: El Cid's service to the Muslim king of Zaragoza: Historia Roderici (c.1110) — primary source. The parias system (tribute paid by taifas to Christian kings): documented in royal archives. Fletcher (1989), The Quest for El Cid, ch.5–6. The cross-religious alliances: O'Callaghan (2003), Reconquest and Crusade in Medieval Spain — argues the religious framing was always mixed with political calculation.

entries.label: Cluny and the Camino — French monasticism transforms Iberia
entries.loc: Cluniac monasteries along the Camino; Burgos; León
entries.hint: The Cluny monastery reforms spread along the Camino — Alfonso VI invites Cluniac monks to reform Spanish monasteries; French Romanesque architecture arrives with the pilgrims; the French Way brings not just pilgrims but settlers, craftsmen and a new European cultural contact.
entries.note: Alfonso VI's Cluny donations: documented in Archivo de la Catedral de Toledo and Cluny's own archives. The Romanesque architecture spread: documented in the Pilgrim's Guide c.1139. Bishko (1969), 'Fernando I and the Origins of the Leonese-Castilian Alliance with Cluny': the standard study. The broader cultural exchange: documented in the building programme at Santiago Cathedral.

entries.label: Council of Burgos 1080 — Mozarabic rite abolished, Europe aligned
entries.hint: At the behest of Rome and the Cluniac reform movement, Alfonso VI convenes the Council of Burgos (1080) and formally abolishes the ancient Mozarabic/Visigothic liturgical rite in favour of the Roman Liturgy — the most consequential Exchange entry for medieval Spain: it permanently aligns Spain's religious, intellectual and cultural exchange with the European mainstream and away from its indigenous late-antique heritage.
entries.note: Council of Burgos 1080: documented in chronicles and in Alfonso VI's royal correspondence. The papal pressure: Gregory VII's letters to Alfonso VI demanding liturgical conformity — documented. The Cluniac role: Bishko (1969), 'Fernando I and the Origins of the Leonese-Castilian Alliance with Cluny'. The replacement of Visigothic script with Caroline minuscule (a direct consequence): Millares Carlo (1983). The Mozarabic rite's survival in Toledo (uniquely preserved by a Toledo privilege): documented — still celebrated in Toledo Cathedral's Mozarabic Chapel.

entries.label: Council of Burgos (1080) — abolition of the Mozarabic rite
entries.hint: Under Cluniac pressure and papal instruction, Alfonso VI formally abolishes the ancient Mozarabic/Visigothic liturgy at the Council of Burgos (1080) and imposes the Roman rite — permanently aligning the Spanish church with the European mainland; the same reform introduces Caroline minuscule script, replacing the Visigothic script in Spanish monasteries.
entries.note: Council of Burgos 1080: documented in contemporary chronicles and later legal collections. The Cluniac role: Bishko (1969), 'Fernando I and the Origins of the Leonese-Castilian Alliance with Cluny': uses Cluny's own archive. The replacement of Visigothic by Caroline script: Millares Carlo (1983) — the palaeographic documentation. Linehan (1993): the standard critical analysis of the reform's ecclesiastical politics. The survival of the Mozarabic rite in Toledo (by special dispensation): documented — still celebrated at Toledo's Mozarabic Chapel today.

entries.label: Crusading integration — Reconquista becomes a papal crusade
entries.hint: Popes grant crusading status to Iberian campaigns — Pope Eugene III's bull for the conquest of Almería (1147) and Innocent III's bull for Las Navas de Tolosa (1212) grant the same spiritual indulgences and financial privileges as crusades to the Holy Land; the Reconquista transforms from a local territorial expansion into an internationally funded and legitimised papal crusade.
entries.note: Pope Eugene III's crusading bull for Almería 1147: Vatican Archives; analysed in O'Callaghan (2003), Reconquest and Crusade ch.2. Innocent III's bull for Las Navas 1212: Vatican Archives. The financial consequences: crusade taxes (twentieth and fortieth) collected across Europe for the Iberian campaigns — documented in papal registers. O'Callaghan (2003): the standard study of the Reconquista as crusade. The integration into the broader crusading movement: Spanish knights receive the same plenary indulgences as Jerusalem crusaders.

categories.label: World
categories.subtitle: The Iberian kingdoms in European and Mediterranean context

entries.label: The Almoravids and Almohads — North Africa intervenes
entries.hint: The fragmented taifa kingdoms invite the Almoravid Berbers from Morocco in 1086 — they reverse Christian gains and subjugate the taifas; then the even stricter Almohad movement succeeds them; the North African interventions transform al-Andalus and eventually provoke the coalition that wins at Las Navas.
entries.note: The Almoravid invitation 1086: Ibn Idhari, al-Bayan al-Mughrib. The Almohad invasion 1147: documented in Arabic sources. The Battle of Alarcos (1195) — Almohad victory over Alfonso VIII: documented in De Rebus Hispaniae. Lomax (1978), The Reconquista of Spain: the standard survey. The paradox: the taifas' invitation of the Almoravids was a strategic mistake — the Almoravids quickly deposed the taifa rulers they came to protect.

entries.label: Crusading integration — papal bulls for the Reconquista
entries.hint: Popes Eugene III (1147) and Innocent III (1212) issue papal crusading bulls for Spanish campaigns — granting the same spiritual privileges as the Levantine Crusades; the Almería campaign of 1147 and Las Navas de Tolosa (1212) are formally integrated into the European crusading movement, transforming local Iberian wars into internationally funded and spiritually validated geopolitical crusades.
entries.note: Eugene III's crusade bull Divina dispensatione (1147): Vatican Archives. Innocent III's crusade bull for Las Navas (1212): Vatican Archives. O'Callaghan (2003), Reconquest and Crusade in Medieval Spain: the standard study — analyses all papal bull evidence. The practical consequences: European knights participating under crusading vows documented in multiple national archives. The spiritual equivalence: explicitly stated in the papal documents. This is the key entry for understanding the Reconquista as a geopolitical phenomenon rather than purely a domestic war.

entries.label: Battle of the Strait — the Marinid geopolitical choke-point
entries.hint: Control of the Strait of Gibraltar is the defining geopolitical contest of late medieval Iberia — Castile, Aragon, Granada and the Marinids of Morocco fight for the crossing points; whoever controls the Strait determines whether North African forces can reinforce the Iberian frontier; the Castilian victory at Río Salado (1340) permanently closes the Strait to North African armies.
entries.note: The 'Battle of the Strait' (Batalla del Estrecho): documented in the Crónica de Alfonso XI. The Marinid interventions 1275–1340: documented in Ibn Khaldun's Muqaddima and in Castilian chronicles. O'Callaghan (2011), The Gibraltar Crusade: the standard modern study of the Strait as geopolitical choke-point. The fortifications at Algeciras, Tarifa and Gibraltar: archaeological and documentary evidence. The closure of the Strait after 1340: no subsequent North African force crosses — documented through absence.

categories.label: Lives
categories.subtitle: Gender, sexuality & marginalised experience · evidence anchored

entries.label: Cautivos — frontier captives and the alfaqueques who ransomed them
entries.loc: The Christian-Muslim frontier (fluid, running through central Iberia)
entries.hint: Living on the fluid frontier between Christian and Muslim Iberia meant constant risk of abduction during lightning raids (razias) — captives were enslaved or held for ransom; specialised trans-frontier negotiators (alfaqueques) emerged to broker ransoms; the Order of Mercy (Mercedarians, founded 1218) was established specifically to ransom impoverished Christian captives from Muslim prisons.
entries.note: The alfaqueque institution: documented in Alfonso X's Siete Partidas (Part II, Title XXIX) — the only medieval European legal code to formally define and regulate trans-frontier ransom negotiators. The Mercedarian Order (1218): papal confirmation 1235 — Vatican Archives. Brodman (1986), Ransoming Captives in Crusader Spain: the standard study. Captivity narratives: preserved in hagiographic sources. The frontier's two-way captivity: Christians in Muslim hands and Muslims in Christian hands — documented symmetrically in Arabic and Latin sources.

entries.label: Queens regent and 'reinas propietarias' — ruling women of medieval Iberia
entries.hint: Urraca of León (1109–1126) is one of medieval Europe's earliest queens regnant — but her early reign included a coerced marriage to Alfonso I of Aragon ('the Battler') who assumed the joint title of King of Castile and León; violent civil war between their factions until papal annulment c.1112–1114 preceded her independent rule; her charters document executive authority clearly from c.1114 onwards.
entries.note: Queen Urraca's charters (1109–1126): Archivo de la Catedral de León and AHN — multiple charters in her own name. The forced marriage to Alfonso I of Aragon: documented in chronicles and in the subsequent papal annulment proceedings. Reilly (1982), The Kingdom of León-Castilla under Queen Urraca 1109–1126: the standard biography — covers both the joint reign and her independent rule after 1114. The papal annulment c.1112–1114: documented in ecclesiastical sources.

entries.label: Jews under Christian rule — fueros and protection
entries.loc: Toledo; Burgos; León; Barcelona (main Jewish communities)
entries.hint: The Jewish communities of Christian Iberia live under royal protection — the fuero system grants them specific rights; Alfonso VI's Toledo is deliberately multi-religious after the reconquest (1085); the community produces scholars in both Hebrew and Arabic, connecting the Sephardic world to European learning.
entries.note: Alfonso VI's Toledo charters for Jews (post-1085): Archivo de la Catedral de Toledo. The Barcelona Jewish community: documented in municipal records from 10th century. Baer (1961–1966), A History of the Jews in Christian Spain (2 vols): the standard study. The contrast with Visigothic persecution: a genuine improvement documented in the charters. The vulnerability before the 1391 pogroms: foreshadowed in the 1109 Toledo pogrom after Alfonso VI's death.
